items.title: Mixture of Experts in Large Language Models
items.author: Victor Leung
items.content:
The rapid evolution of large language models (LLMs) has brought unprecedented capabilities to artificial intelligence, but it has also introduced significant challenges in computational cost, scalability, and efficiency. The (MoE) architecture has emerged as a groundbreaking solution to these challenges, enabling LLMs to scale efficiently while maintaining high performance. This blog post explores the concept, workings, benefits, and challenges of MoE in LLMs.

What is Mixture of Experts (MoE)?

The Mixture of Experts approach divides a neural network into specialized sub-networks called "experts," each trained to handle specific subsets of input data or tasks. A gating network dynamically routes inputs to the most relevant experts based on the problem at hand. Unlike traditional dense models where all parameters are activated for every input, MoE selectively activates only a subset of experts, optimizing computational efficiency.

This architecture is inspired by ensemble methods in machine learning but introduces dynamic routing mechanisms that allow the model to specialize in different domains or tasks. For example, one expert might excel at syntax processing while another focuses on semantic understanding.

MoE operates through two main phases: training and inference.

  1. : Each expert specializes in a distinct subset of data or task, refining its capabilities to address specific challenges.
  2. : The gating network learns to route inputs to the most suitable experts by optimizing a probability distribution over all experts.
  3. : Both experts and the gating network are trained collaboratively using a combined loss function to ensure harmony between task assignment and overall performance.
  1. : The gating network evaluates incoming data and assigns it to relevant experts.
  2. : Only the most pertinent experts are activated for each input, minimizing resource usage.
  3. : Outputs from activated experts are merged into a unified result using techniques like weighted averaging.

Advantages of MoE in LLMs

MoE offers several key benefits that make it particularly effective for large-scale AI applications:

  • : By activating only relevant experts for each task, MoE reduces unnecessary computation and accelerates inference.
  • : MoE allows models to scale to trillions of parameters without proportional increases in computational costs.
  • : Experts focus on specific tasks or domains, improving accuracy and adaptability across diverse applications like multilingual translation and text summarization.
  • : New experts can be added or existing ones modified without disrupting the overall model architecture.
  • : The modular nature ensures that issues with one expert do not compromise the entire system's functionality.

Challenges in Implementing MoE

Despite its advantages, MoE comes with significant challenges:

  1. : Coordinating the gating network with multiple experts requires sophisticated optimization techniques.

    Hyperparameter tuning is more demanding due to the increased complexity of the architecture.

  2. : Routing inputs through the gating network adds computational steps.

    Activating multiple experts simultaneously can strain memory and parallelism capabilities.

  3. Infrastructure Requirements: Sparse models demand substantial memory during execution as all experts need to be stored.

    Deployment on edge devices or resource-constrained environments requires additional engineering efforts.

  4. : Ensuring uniform workload distribution among experts is critical for optimal performance but challenging to achieve.

Applications of MoE in LLMs

MoE is transforming various fields by enabling efficient handling of complex tasks:

Natural Language Processing (NLP)

  • Multilingual Models: Experts specialize in language-specific tasks, enabling efficient translation across dozens of languages (e.g., Microsoft Z-code).
  • Text Summarization & Question Answering: Task-specific routing enhances accuracy by leveraging domain-specialized experts.
  • Vision Transformers (ViTs): Google’s V-MoEs dynamically route image patches to specialized experts for improved recognition accuracy and speed.

State-of-the-Art Models Using MoE

Several cutting-edge LLMs employ MoE architectures:

  • OpenAI’s GPT-4 reportedly integrates MoE techniques for enhanced scalability and efficiency.
  • Mistral AI’s Mixtral 8x7B model leverages MoE for faster inference and reduced computational costs.
  • Google’s Gemini 1.5 and IBM’s Granite 3.0 showcase innovative applications of MoE in multi-modal AI systems.

The Mixture of Experts architecture is poised for further innovation:

  • Enhanced routing algorithms for better load balancing and inference efficiency.
  • Integration with multi-modal systems combining text, images, and other data types.
  • Democratization through open-source implementations like DeepSeek R1, making advanced AI accessible to a broader audience.

Mixture of Experts represents a paradigm shift in how large language models are designed and deployed. By combining specialization with scalability, it addresses key limitations of traditional dense architectures while unlocking new possibilities for AI applications across domains. As research continues to refine this approach, MoE is set to play a pivotal role in shaping the future of artificial intelligence.

items.title: The Bot Assistant??
items.author: Favour Chikezie
items.content:
Are bots really the assistants, or have we become the assistants?

Introduction: The Shift We Didn’t See Coming

At first, AI and automation were just tools designed to make life easier. They handled routine tasks, cut down on manual labor, and helped businesses scale. But something interesting has happened over time.

Now, AI isn’t just assisting us; it’s making many of the decisions. In fields like data analysis, software development, customer service, marketing, and even creative industries, AI isn’t just a tool, it’s becoming the main worker, while humans are left maintaining and adjusting its performance.

So, that raises an important question:

“Are bots really the assistants, or have we gradually become the assistants?”

Nowhere is this shift more evident than in data analysis, where automation has changed the game entirely. Let’s break this down.

AI in Data Analysis: Who’s Really Doing the Work?

1.AI Now Handles Data Collection & Cleaning: Before AI, data analysts spent hours collecting, sorting, and cleaning messy datasets before they could even begin analyzing.

Now, AI tools like OpenRefine, Trifacta, and AutoML automatically clean, structure, and categorize data. Instead of data analysts doing the grunt work, they now just oversee AI as it organizes and prepares data in minutes.

Question: If AI can do this faster and more accurately, what’s left for human data analysts?

2.AI Detects Trends & Patterns Faster Than Humans: Traditionally, data analysts had to manually study trends, look for correlations, and build predictive models to forecast outcomes.

Now, AI-powered tools like Google Cloud AutoML, Tableau AI, and Microsoft Azure AI can do this instantly.

• AI doesn’t just analyze trends, it predicts future outcomes with astonishing accuracy.

• AI-powered systems can scan millions of data points in seconds, something that would take a human days or weeks.

• Businesses rely more on AI-generated insights because they are data-driven and eliminate human bias.

Question: If businesses trust AI’s insights more than a human’s, what does that mean for the role of a data analyst?

3.AI Is Now Writing Reports & Making Business Decisions: There was a time when only humans could interpret data and explain its significance to business leaders.

That was a key role of analysts, But now, AI-generated reports are everywhere.

• AI-powered tools like Narrative Science, Tableau Explain Data, and IBM Watson Analytics now generate full reports with insights, explanations, and even suggested business decisions.

• Instead of asking an analyst, executives can now simply ask an AI assistant, “What are our top-selling products this month?” and get an instant answer.

• AI doesn’t just analyze data, it provides recommendations based on that data.

Question: If AI can generate reports and offer strategic advice, do executives even need human analysts anymore?

Humans vs. AI: Who’s Really in Control?

The data analyst’s role has shifted drastically from doing the work to supervising AI as it does the work. Instead of spending hours on Excel sheets, analysts now:

• Fine-tune AI models to improve their accuracy

• Validate AI-generated insights to ensure they align with business needs

• Interpret AI recommendations and apply human intuition when needed

But here’s the thing, as AI gets smarter, the need for human supervision is decreasing. The more we train AI to work independently, the less it relies on us.

So, it’s worth asking: Are we training AI, or is AI training us to work around it?

Are We Becoming AI’s Assistants? Think about how work has changed:

• Data analysts now spend more time adjusting AI models than actually analyzing raw data.

• Developers use AI to write code, while they just tweak it.

• Marketers use AI to generate ads, while they just select the best one.

• Customer support reps monitor AI chatbots, stepping in only when needed.

This pattern repeats across industries. Instead of doing the work, we’re increasingly just managing AI while it does the work for us.

And that leads to the bigger question:

As AI takes over more tasks, where do humans fit into the future of work? The Future of Work: Adapt or Be Replaced?

So, what’s next? Will AI completely replace humans in fields like data analysis, web development, and marketing? Not exactly. But the roles will continue to change.

How to Stay Relevant in the Age of AI

• Master AI-Driven Tools: The more you understand AI, the better you can work with it. Learn how to train, fine-tune, and optimize AI models in your field.

• Focus on Creativity & Critical Thinking: AI is powerful, but it still lacks human intuition, ethical reasoning, and emotional intelligence. These are the skills that will always set you apart.

• Become an AI Supervisor, Not a Worker: Instead of competing with AI, learn how to manage AI-driven workflows and ensure their accuracy.

• Develop Hybrid Skills: The best professionals will be a mix of technical and strategic thinkers, able to work with AI while applying a human touch.

Final Thoughts: Who’s Really in Charge?

At the start, we asked: Are bots really the assistants, or have we become the assistants?

The answer isn’t black and white. AI is here to stay, and it’s doing an incredible job at making work faster, smarter, and more efficient. But as automation increases, the human role is shifting from “worker” to “overseer.”

If you want to stay ahead, you need to evolve with AI, not compete against it. The future belongs to those who can work alongside AI, making it better, smarter, and more aligned with human goals.

So, what do you think?

• Is AI an opportunity or a threat to your job?

• How is AI changing your field?

• What are you doing to adapt?

Drop your thoughts below!

items.title: Elevate Your Social Media with Creative Fonts
items.author: Julia Rodyk
items.content:
In today’s digital world, standing out on social media is more difficult than ever. With so much content being shared every minute, it’s easy for your posts to get lost in the noise. To grab attention, using unique and visually appealing fonts is key. Eye-catching typography can make your posts more engaging and memorable, helping them stand out on platforms like Instagram.

If you want to easily enhance your Instagram posts, the Instagram Fonts Generator on Creative Fabrica is the perfect tool. It allows you to generate custom fonts for your captions, stories, or any other content on Instagram. With just a few clicks, you can choose from a variety of creative font styles to make your posts more visually appealing without needing any design skills.

Creative Fabrica offers a wide range of resources to help you create stunning content for social media. With thousands of fonts and design tools at your fingertips, it’s a one-stop shop to make your Instagram and other social media content truly unique.

items.author: Yiğit Konur
items.content:

I've used my research agents to bring all relevant information about config.yaml of LiteLLM to make it comprehensive guide that can be useful for most of people in here. Also useful for copy-paste and discuss with AIs like o1-pro while building config files. If you need quick copy paste to tools like Claude / Thinkbuddy to discuss that, visit my Gist and click 'Raw' button to make it easy to copy paste: https://gist.github.com/yigitkonur/4053c1e31f9351da1d91a0ca410442fd

                Understanding Structure

                The file serves as the central configuration hub for the LiteLLM Proxy Server. It's a YAML-formatted file that consolidates all the settings needed to define how your proxy will operate, including: which LLM models you'll use, how to connect to them, how to route requests, how to manage authentication, and how to customize various aspects of the proxy's behavior. A well-structured and understood is for a functional and secure LiteLLM Proxy deployment.

                The file is organized into several top-level sections, each responsible for a specific area of configuration. These sections are:

                • (Optional): This section allows you to define environment variables the file. This can be convenient for managing credentials and endpoints within the configuration itself, especially in deployment environments like Kubernetes, where managing external environment variables might be more complex.

                • (Required): This is a that defines all the LLMs and model deployments that your LiteLLM Proxy will manage and serve. It's where you specify models you want to use, their backend configurations (provider, API keys, endpoints, etc.), and any associated metadata like cost information or context window limits.

                • : This section contains general settings that configure the behavior of the core the proxy. These are settings that apply broadly across the proxy, influencing things like logging, timeouts, fallback mechanisms, and caching behavior.

                • (Optional): This section lets you fine-tune the behavior of specific logging and monitoring callbacks that you've enabled in . For example, you can use it to control message logging for the OpenTelemetry () callback.

                • : This section encompasses proxy server-level settings. These are configurations that apply to the proxy server , including settings for the master key, database connections, authentication methods (like JWT or OAuth2), global rate limits, alerting, and various feature flags.

                • : This section focuses on the proxy's routing and request handling configurations. It includes settings for choosing the load balancing strategy (e.g., round-robin, least-busy, latency-based), defining retry policies, setting concurrency limits, and configuring how fallbacks to alternative models should work.

                Key Principles to Keep in Mind:

                • The file must be a valid YAML file. YAML is a human-readable data serialization format that uses indentation (spaces, not tabs) to define structure.
                • Environment Variable Overrides: Most configuration values in can also be set via . If a setting is defined both in and as an environment variable, the YAML setting typically takes precedence (except where noted in this guide).
                • Settings defined in generally override similar settings in for router-specific operations. This allows for fine-grained control over the router's behavior.
                • Changes to generally require restarting the LiteLLM Proxy Server for the changes to take effect.

                Section: Defining Variables within YAML

                Mapping (String to String)

                This section provides a way to define environment variables your file. While you can always set environment variables externally in your operating system or deployment environment, the section offers some convenience, especially in certain contexts:

                • Centralized Configuration: It allows you to keep all your configuration settings, including environment variables, in a single, centralized file.
                • This is particularly helpful in Kubernetes deployments, where you might prefer to define environment variables within your configuration files (e.g., ConfigMaps or Secrets) rather than setting them separately in the Kubernetes environment.
                • For some deployments, managing environment variables within the can simplify setup and configuration.

                Each entry under is a key-value pair:

                • The name of the environment variable (e.g., , ).
                • The string value to be assigned to that environment variable.

                In this example, the LiteLLM Proxy will operate as if the environment variables , , and were set externally in the system environment.

                Referencing Defined Environment Variables:

                Within parts of your file, you can these defined environment variables using the syntax: . This tells LiteLLM to fetch the actual value from the environment (either defined in the section or externally).

                Here, "os.environ/OPENAI_API_KEY" will be replaced with the value of the environment variable (either from the example above, or a value set externally if one exists).

                If an environment variable is defined in the section externally in your system environment, the YAML setting (within ) typically takes precedence.

                Section: Defining Models and Deployments

                The section is and forms the core of your LiteLLM Proxy configuration. It's where you define all the LLMs and model deployments that your proxy will manage and make accessible to your applications. Each entry in the represents a single model or model alias that the proxy will serve.

                Structure of a Entry:

                Each entry in the is a that contains the following key components:

                • (String, Required): This is the user-friendly or name that client applications will use to refer to this specific model deployment when making API requests to the proxy. This is the name your application code will use.
                • (Object, Required): This is a that contains the core parameters that the LiteLLM library will use to actually interact with the backend LLM API. This is where you specify the model identifier (provider and model name), API keys, base URLs, and other provider-specific settings.
                • (Object, Optional): This is an optional nested mapping that allows you to provide metadata and descriptive attributes about the model, such as cost per token, context window size, and a description. While optional, it's highly recommended to provide this information for monitoring, cost tracking, and management purposes.

                Example Section:

                This example defines model deployments:

                1. : An alias for an Azure OpenAI deployment of GPT-3.5 Turbo. The specify the necessary details to connect to the Azure OpenAI service, including the deployment name, API base URL, API key (loaded from an environment variable), and API version. The section provides metadata like the context window size () and estimated costs per token.
                2. : An alias for a locally served LLaMA 2 model (presumably using an OpenAI-compatible interface like vLLM). The use the prefix to indicate an OpenAI-compatible API, and the points to a local server URL. No API key is needed ().

                Key Concepts within :

                • The acts as an alias. Your client applications use this alias when making API requests, without needing to know the specific backend provider or deployment details. This abstraction allows you to change the backend model without modifying your application code, as long as the remains consistent.
                • The value (e.g., , , ) tells LiteLLM provider's API to use for this model. The proxy uses this to route requests to the correct backend.
                • It's a best practice to use environment variables (referenced via ) to store sensitive information like API keys, rather than hardcoding them directly in the file.
                • for Transparency: The section is highly recommended for transparency and manageability. It provides valuable metadata that can be used for monitoring, cost tracking, and displaying model information in the proxy's Admin UI (if enabled).

                Model List Parameter Summary

                User-facing alias for the model.
                LiteLLM parameters for backend API calls.
                Metadata about the model (cost, context window, etc.).

                N/A (Required Parameter)

                The parameter defines the or name of a model deployment within your LiteLLM Proxy configuration. This is the name that client applications will use when making API requests to the proxy. It acts as a logical identifier, allowing you to abstract away the complexities of backend model names and provider-specific details. Each within the must be unique.

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only parameter)

                N/A (Required Parameter)

                The section is a within each entry. It contains the core configuration parameters that the LiteLLM library uses to interact with the actual backend LLM API. These parameters dictate LiteLLM will call the underlying model provider. At a minimum, it include the parameter, but can include a wide range of settings to customize API calls.

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only section header)

                N/A (Required Parameter within )

                The parameter, nested within , is and specifies the actual backend model identifier. This string tells LiteLLM LLM provider to use and from that provider to target. The format of this string is provider-specific, and typically follows the pattern "provider_name/model_identifier". For example:

                • : Specifies OpenAI's GPT-3.5 Turbo model.
                • "azure/gpt-turbo-small-eu": Specifies an Azure OpenAI deployment named "gpt-turbo-small-eu".
                • "anthropic/claude-instant-1": Specifies Anthropic's Claude Instant-1 model.

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only parameter)

                Corresponding environment variable depends on the provider (e.g., , , , etc.)

                N/A (Required for most providers)

                The parameter, within , is used to provide the API key or authentication token required to access the backend LLM provider's API. Whether this parameter is strictly depends on the specific provider and model. Many commercial providers (like OpenAI, Anthropic, Azure) require an API key for authentication. It's highly recommended to API keys directly in your file. Instead, use environment variable references like "os.environ/API_KEY_NAME" to fetch the key from environment variables for better security and easier management.

                Example Environment Variable:

                Corresponding environment variable depends on the provider (e.g., , custom endpoint environment variables).

                Provider-specific default base URL.

                The parameter, under , allows you to specify a for the LLM provider's API endpoint. This is often necessary when you are not using the default public endpoint of a provider. Common use cases include:

                • Azure OpenAI deployments require a custom URL that points to your specific Azure endpoint.
                • Open-Source Model Gateways: When using open-source models served through gateways like vLLM or custom inference servers, you'll need to set to the URL of your gateway.
                • Non-Default Regions or Endpoints: Some providers might offer APIs in different regions or with custom endpoints, requiring you to override the default .

                If is not provided, LiteLLM will use a provider-specific default base URL where applicable.

                Example Environment Variable:

                Provider-Specific Settings (within )

                Provider-specific parameter names (e.g., , , , etc.)

                Depends on the parameter (String, Integer, Boolean, etc.)

                Corresponding environment variable depends on the provider and parameter.

                Provider-specific defaults.

                Beyond the common parameters like and , the section can include provider-specific settings. These parameters are unique to each LLM provider and are used to configure provider-specific behaviors or features. Examples include:

                • (Azure OpenAI): Specifies the API version to use for Azure OpenAI deployments.
                • (AWS Bedrock): Sets the AWS region for Bedrock models.
                • (Google Vertex AI): Specifies the Google Cloud Project ID for Vertex AI models.
                • (Azure OpenAI): Used to target a specific deployment within Azure OpenAI.

                Refer to the "Providers" section of this documentation and the official documentation of each LLM provider to identify available provider-specific parameters and their usage.

                Example Environment Variable:

                ()

                Number (Float or Integer)

                Provider-specific default (often 1.0 for OpenAI, but can vary).

                The parameter, within , controls the of the LLM's output. It's a common parameter across many LLM providers, though the range and interpretation might slightly vary.

                • Lower values (e.g., 0.1, 0.0): Result in more deterministic, focused, and predictable output. The model will tend to choose the most likely next tokens, making it suitable for tasks requiring factual accuracy or code generation.
                • Higher values (e.g., 0.7, 1.0): Introduce more randomness and creativity into the output. The model will consider a wider range of possible next tokens, leading to more surprising, diverse, and potentially less predictable text. Useful for creative writing, brainstorming, or tasks where originality is valued over strict accuracy.

                A of makes the output almost entirely deterministic (always choosing the most likely token), while values approaching (or sometimes higher, depending on provider) maximize randomness.

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only parameter)

                ()

                Provider-specific default (often varies significantly by model).

                The parameter, in , sets a limit on the maximum number of tokens the LLM should generate in its response. This is a crucial parameter for controlling output length, latency, and cost.

                • specifies the on the generated text length. The LLM may stop generating before reaching this limit if it naturally completes its response (e.g., reaches a stop sequence or end-of-text token).
                • Limiting is a direct way to control the cost of LLM API calls, as you are billed per token.
                • Shorter values generally result in lower latency, as the model has less text to generate.
                • Context Window Consideration: Be mindful of the model's maximum context window when setting . The sum of input tokens (prompt) and should not exceed the model's context limit.

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only parameter)

                No default (no model-level RPM limit unless explicitly set).

                The parameter, within , sets a requests-per-minute (RPM) rate limit specifically for this particular model deployment. This is a deployment-level rate limit, distinct from global or per-key rate limits configured elsewhere.

                • Model-Specific Rate Limiting: The limit applies only to the model deployment in which it is defined. It does not affect other models or deployments in your .
                • helps manage concurrency at the model level, preventing overload of specific backend deployments, especially slower or resource-intensive models.
                • Use to control the rate of requests to different models based on their capacity, cost, or priority.

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only parameter)

                No default (no model-level TPM limit unless explicitly set).

                The parameter, under , defines a tokens-per-minute (TPM) rate limit specifically for . Similar to , this is a deployment-level limit, separate from global or per-key TPM limits.

                • Token-Based Rate Limiting: limits the total number of tokens that can be processed by this model deployment within a minute. This includes both input (prompt) tokens and output (completion) tokens.
                • provides finer-grained control over usage compared to , as it accounts for the length and complexity of requests, not just the number of requests.
                • Use to manage costs more precisely, particularly for models with variable token pricing or when you want to limit total token consumption for a deployment.

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only parameter)

                No default (optional section).

                The section is an within each entry. It allows you to provide metadata and descriptive attributes about the model deployment. While not strictly required for the proxy to function, is highly recommended for:

                • Monitoring and Observability: Provides data points for dashboards and monitoring systems.
                • Enables accurate cost calculations and spend analysis.
                • Model Management UI (Admin UI): Populates information in the proxy's Admin UI (if enabled) for model browsing and documentation.
                • Data in is surfaced via the proxy's endpoint, allowing clients to programmatically retrieve model metadata.

                can include fields like cost per token, context window size, model mode (completion, embedding, etc.), and custom metadata.

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only section header)

                If not provided, LiteLLM may auto-generate a unique ID, but explicit definition is recommended for consistency.

                The parameter, within , allows you to specify a for the model deployment. While technically optional, providing a custom is for:

                • Ensures a stable, human-readable identifier for the model, especially when managing multiple deployments or versions.
                • Makes it easier to track and analyze logs and metrics associated with a specific model instance.
                • Provides a clear identifier in the proxy's Admin UI and endpoint.

                If you don't provide an , LiteLLM may automatically generate one, but relying on auto-generated IDs can make management and tracking less predictable.

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only parameter)

                If not provided, LiteLLM may attempt to infer the mode based on the model name, but explicit definition is recommended.

                The parameter, within , specifies the of the model deployment. This informs the proxy about how to handle the model, particularly for tasks like health checks and API routing. Common values include:

                • : For chat completion and text completion models (most common).
                • : For embedding models.
                • : For image generation models.
                • : For audio transcription models.

                Setting correctly is important for features like health checks, which may perform mode-specific validations (e.g., health checks for image models expect ). While the proxy might the mode in some cases, it's best practice to the for clarity and robustness.

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only parameter)

                ()

                Number (Float or Integer)

                If not provided, LiteLLM may use a default cost from its internal model cost map, if available for the model.

                The parameter, within , specifies the cost in USD (or your chosen currency) per 1,000 input tokens for this model deployment. This is used for internal cost tracking and reporting within LiteLLM Proxy.

                • Providing accurate cost data is crucial for precise spend tracking and budget enforcement.
                • Use this parameter to define custom pricing if the default cost map is inaccurate or if you have negotiated specific pricing with your provider.
                • Costs are always interpreted in USD (or your chosen base currency) and are typically represented as a very small decimal number (e.g., for $0.0015 per 1,000 tokens).

                If is not provided, LiteLLM may attempt to use a default cost from its internal model cost map. However, for accurate cost tracking, it's recommended to explicitly define both and .

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only parameter)

                ()

                Number (Float or Integer)

                If not provided, LiteLLM may use a default cost from its internal model cost map, if available for the model.

                The parameter, within , defines the cost in USD (or your chosen currency) per 1,000 output tokens generated by this model deployment. This is used in conjunction with for comprehensive cost tracking.

                • You should always provide and to get a full picture of the cost implications of using a model.
                • Different models and providers may have varying costs for input and output tokens. Ensure you configure these values accurately based on your provider's pricing.

                If is omitted, LiteLLM might attempt to use a default cost from its internal model cost map. However, for reliable cost tracking, it's recommended to explicitly set both input and output cost parameters.

                Section: Configuring the LiteLLM Library

                This section allows you to configure the behavior of the underlying LiteLLM Python library within the proxy. These settings globally influence logging, networking, caching, and fallback behaviors. Many of these settings correspond to functions within the module or internal flags. While most values can also be set via environment variables (prefixed with ), defining them in under is often more convenient. If a setting is defined in both and , the value will take precedence specifically for router operations.

                Logging & Callback Settings

                This subsection within allows you to configure how the proxy handles logging and integrates with various monitoring and observability platforms. You can define callbacks for successful and failed requests, enable detailed debugging, and control what information is logged.

                The parameter, within , is a that defines service health monitoring callbacks. These callbacks are triggered by individual LLM API requests. Instead, they are designed to capture internal service-level events and failures within the LiteLLM Proxy itself, such as:

                • Database Connection Issues: Errors connecting to or interacting with the proxy's database (Postgres).
                • Problems with Redis, S3, or other caching backends.
                • Exceptions or failures within the proxy's own components or services.

                are typically used for operational monitoring of the proxy's health and infrastructure, rather than for logging individual LLM request details. Examples include and for reporting internal metrics and errors to monitoring platforms.

                (Message logging is enabled by default).

                The parameter, in , is a that controls whether the content of prompts (messages) and responses are logged to your configured callbacks.

                • Setting turn_off_message_logging: true prevents the actual text of conversations from being logged to external logging/monitoring systems. This is crucial for privacy-sensitive applications or when dealing with confidential data in prompts and responses.
                • Disabling message logging can significantly reduce the volume of logs generated by the proxy, which can be beneficial for cost optimization and easier log management, especially in high-throughput environments.
                • Metadata Logging Still Active: Even when message logging is turned off, the proxy will still log about requests, such as token counts, costs, timestamps, model names, and error details. Only the actual text content of prompts and responses is suppressed.

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only parameter)

                In code, you can achieve the same effect by setting litellm.turn_off_message_logging=True globally in your application or proxy initialization code.

                (User API key information is logged by default).

                The parameter, within , is a that controls whether information related to the end-user's API key (virtual key) is redacted (removed or anonymized) from logs sent to your configured callbacks.

                • Privacy of User Credentials: When enabled (redact_user_api_key_info: true), the proxy will attempt to remove or anonymize user-specific credential information from logs before they are sent to external logging systems (like Langfuse, OpenTelemetry, etc.). This includes:
                  • The hashed virtual API key itself.
                  • User IDs, team IDs, or any other identifiers associated with the virtual key.
                • This feature helps prevent accidental exposure of user-specific credential information in external logs, enhancing privacy and security, especially in multi-tenant environments where user API keys are used.
                • Redaction is supported for specific loggers, including Langfuse, OpenTelemetry, Logfire, and ArizeAI (as of the documentation). Check the official documentation for the most up-to-date list of supported loggers.

                • Enhanced Langfuse Filtering and Analysis: Tags are key-value pairs that can be attached to log events in Langfuse. By defining default tags, you can automatically enrich your Langfuse logs with useful metadata, making it easier to filter, group, and analyze requests within the Langfuse platform.
                • Common tags you might want to include by default could be:
                  • : The base URL of your LiteLLM Proxy instance.
                  • : The alias or identifier of the user API key used for the request.
                  • : Any semantic similarity scores or metrics calculated during request processing.
                  • : Indicates if the response was served from the cache.
                  • : The User ID associated with the API key.

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only parameter)

                These tags will then be automatically applied to every log event sent to Langfuse by the proxy, aiding in filtering and analysis within the Langfuse UI.

                (Parameter modification is disabled by default).

                The parameter, within , is an that enables a powerful, but potentially risky, feature: allowing programmatic modification of request parameters the proxy sends the request to the backend LLM provider.

                • Custom Request Transformations: When , the proxy allows custom code (typically via a plugin or callback) to intercept and alter the dictionary or the request body itself . This opens up possibilities for highly customized request handling.
                • is primarily intended for advanced and enterprise scenarios, such as:
                  • Implementing complex plugins that need to dynamically adjust API requests based on real-time conditions, user context, or external data.
                  • Provider-Specific Parameter Injection: Programmatically injecting provider-specific parameters that are not directly exposed through standard LiteLLM parameters, but needed for certain backend models.
                  • Adding extra information or context to the request payload based on custom logic.

                Important Security and Stability Considerations:

                • Enabling introduces significant security risks if not used carefully. Malicious or poorly written custom code could potentially:
                  • Expose sensitive data by logging or transmitting modified requests.
                  • Bypass security checks or rate limits.
                  • Cause unexpected behavior or errors in the proxy or backend LLM.
                • Incorrectly modifying parameters can lead to malformed API requests, causing errors or unpredictable LLM behavior.
                • Advanced Feature – Use with Caution: This parameter is marked as an advanced feature and should be used only if you fully understand the risks and have a clear, well-justified use case. Thoroughly test and audit any custom code that modifies request parameters.

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only parameter)

                The actual logic for parameters are modified is configured via . simply the capability. The custom modification logic itself must be implemented in separate Python code, usually within a plugin or callback, that is designed to work with this feature.

                (Preview features are disabled by default).

                The parameter, within , is a that controls whether experimental or preview features within the LiteLLM library are enabled.

                • Access to New or Experimental Functionality: LiteLLM may introduce new features or integrations that are initially marked as "preview" or "experimental". These features are typically under active development and may have limited stability, documentation, or support.
                • Early Access and Testing: Enabling enable_preview_features: true allows you to access and test these preview features, giving you early access to new capabilities and the opportunity to provide feedback.
                • Potential Instability or Changes: Preview features are considered stable and are subject to change, removal, or movement to the Enterprise edition in future releases. Do not rely on preview features in production environments unless you are prepared for potential instability and changes.
                • Always consult the official LiteLLM documentation to understand features are currently behind the flag and what their limitations or caveats are.

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only parameter)

                At the time of writing, enabling Azure's "O1" series models with streaming requires setting enable_preview_features: true.

                The parameter, within , is a that enables from the core LiteLLM Python library itself. When enabled, LiteLLM will output detailed debug messages to the proxy's console (stdout), providing very granular information about its internal operations, API calls, and processing steps.

                • Detailed Debugging Information: Verbose logging is invaluable for troubleshooting complex issues, understanding the internal workings of LiteLLM, or debugging custom integrations. It can reveal:
                  • Detailed request and response payloads sent to and received from LLM providers.
                  • Internal function calls and execution flow within the LiteLLM library.
                  • Variable values and internal state during request processing.
                • Verbose logging generates a very large volume of log output. Do not enable in production environments unless absolutely necessary for debugging a specific issue, as it can impact performance, consume disk space, and potentially expose sensitive information in logs.
                • Development and Testing Tool: is primarily intended as a development and debugging tool for local testing or controlled staging environments.

                • When , the proxy will output all logs (including request logs, error logs, and other proxy events) in JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) format instead of the default plain text format.
                • Structured Logging Benefits: JSON logs are highly beneficial for:
                  • Log Aggregation and Analysis: JSON format is easily parsed and ingested by log management systems (like Elasticsearch, Splunk, Datadog Logs, etc.), enabling efficient centralized logging, searching, and analysis.
                  • JSON logs are structured data, making it much easier to programmatically parse, filter, and process log information for monitoring dashboards, automated alerts, or custom analytics tools.

                Networking & Timeout Settings

                This subsection of controls network-related configurations, primarily focusing on timeouts and connection behaviors for requests made to the backend LLM providers. These settings are crucial for ensuring responsiveness and handling network issues.

                Integer (representing seconds)

                While the documentation notes 6000 seconds (100 minutes), this seems exceptionally high and might be a typo. A more typical default, based on OpenAI SDK defaults and general API timeout practices, would be closer to or even less. You should test and confirm the actual default behavior in your LiteLLM Proxy version.

                The parameter, within , sets the timeout duration in seconds for all LLM API calls made by the LiteLLM library within the proxy. This timeout applies to the duration of a single API call to the backend LLM provider, including network latency, server-side processing, and response streaming.

                • Preventing Hanging Requests: is crucial for preventing indefinite hanging or stalled requests. If a call to the upstream model takes longer than the specified timeout, the proxy will and raise a error.
                • Timeouts help free up resources on the proxy server and prevent it from getting stuck waiting for unresponsive or slow LLM providers.
                • Setting a reasonable timeout ensures that clients receive timely responses or error indications, rather than waiting indefinitely for a response that may never come.
                • It's generally recommended to set a to a , balancing responsiveness with allowing enough time for legitimate LLM processing. A value like might be a good starting point for many applications, but you should adjust it based on your expected LLM response times and application requirements.

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only parameter)

                There is also a parameter, which sets a timeout for the within the proxy router, including retries and fallbacks. The litellm_settings.request_timeout is specifically for the underlying LLM API call itself. If both are set, typically takes precedence for overall request handling, while litellm_settings.request_timeout governs the individual LLM call timeout.

                (IPv6 is preferred if available, otherwise IPv4 is used).

                The parameter, in , is a that, when set to , forces all outgoing LLM API requests from the proxy to use IPv4 (Internet Protocol version 4) exclusively.

                • IPv6 Compatibility Issues Workaround: In certain network environments or with specific LLM providers, IPv6 connectivity might cause issues, such as HTTPX connection errors (particularly observed with Anthropic API). Setting can act as a workaround by forcing the proxy to use IPv4, potentially resolving these connectivity problems.
                • In environments where IPv6 is not fully supported or reliably configured, forcing IPv4 ensures consistent and predictable network communication for LLM API calls.
                • By default (), the proxy will attempt to use IPv6 if it is available and properly configured in the system environment. If IPv6 is not available or fails, it will fall back to IPv4 automatically. Setting disables this IPv6 preference and forces IPv4-only communication.

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only parameter)

                Only enable if you are experiencing specific IPv6 connectivity issues with your LLM provider APIs. In most modern network environments, leaving it at the default is recommended, allowing the proxy to leverage IPv6 if available.

                forward_traceparent_to_llm_provider

                forward_traceparent_to_llm_provider

                (Traceparent header is not forwarded by default).

                The forward_traceparent_to_llm_provider parameter, within , is a that controls whether the proxy will forward any incoming header it receives from the client application to the .

                • Distributed Tracing Propagation: The header is part of the W3C Trace Context standard for distributed tracing. When a client application initiates a trace (e.g., using OpenTelemetry or a similar tracing system) and includes a header in its request to the proxy, enabling forward_traceparent_to_llm_provider: true will propagate this trace context to the backend LLM provider.
                • This allows for end-to-end traceability of requests across your entire system, from the client application, through the LiteLLM Proxy, and into the LLM provider's infrastructure (if the provider also supports and honors trace context propagation). This is invaluable for debugging and performance monitoring of distributed LLM applications.
                • Self-Hosted or Internal LLMs:forward_traceparent_to_llm_provider: true is generally safe and useful when you are proxying to or that you control or trust and that are designed to handle trace context propagation.
                • Caution with External Services: when enabling this for external, third-party LLM services (like OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Vertex AI, AWS Bedrock). External services may not recognize or correctly handle the header. In some cases, forwarding unknown headers to external services might even cause errors or unexpected behavior. The documentation explicitly warns against using this with external services like AWS Bedrock or Google Vertex AI, as they may reject requests with unknown headers.
                • The default value is for safety, to avoid unintended issues with external services.

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only parameter)

                Only enable forward_traceparent_to_llm_provider: true if you are certain that your LLM backend (typically a self-hosted or internal service) is designed to accept and handle headers. For external, third-party LLM providers, leave this setting at its default unless you have explicit guidance from the provider to enable trace context propagation.

                Fallback & Reliability Settings

                This subsection within provides options for configuring fallback models and other reliability features. These settings help your proxy handle errors and ensure service continuity when primary LLMs are unavailable or exceed context limits.

                Array of Strings (List of model names)

                (Empty list, no default fallbacks configured).

                The parameter, within , is an that defines a . This list specifies which alternative models the proxy should attempt to use as a if a request to the originally intended model (except for ContentPolicyViolationError and ContextWindowExceededError, which have their own dedicated fallback settings).

                • acts as a mechanism. If a request to a model fails and no model-specific fallback is defined (via router_settings.fallbacks or request-level parameter) and the error is not a content policy or context window error, the proxy will try the models listed in in the order they are specified.
                • Reliability and Redundancy: Use to enhance the reliability of your LLM application by ensuring that there are always backup options if primary models become unavailable or encounter issues.
                • Ordered Fallback Attempts: The proxy will attempt to use the fallback models in the list . If the first fallback model also fails, it will try the next one, and so on, until either a fallback succeeds or the list is exhausted.
                • :** The model names listed in must be valid aliases that are defined in your section of .

                In this example, if a request fails for any model and no specific fallback is set for that model, the proxy will first attempt to use the model aliased as . If that also fails, it will then try . If both fallbacks fail, the request will ultimately result in an error.

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only parameter)

                is a fallback mechanism. For more granular control, you can define model-specific fallbacks using router_settings.fallbacks (see Router Settings section).

                Array of Objects (List of mappings)

                (Empty list, no content policy fallbacks configured).

                The parameter, within , is an used to specify fallback models specifically for ContentPolicyViolationError errors. This error typically indicates that the requested content (prompt or completion) violated the content policy of the LLM provider (e.g., OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.).

                • Content Moderation Handling: allows you to define a specific fallback strategy to handle content policy rejections gracefully. Instead of simply returning an error to the client, the proxy can automatically retry the request with a different, potentially less restrictive, model.
                • Model-Specific Fallbacks for Content Policy: You can define fallbacks on a per-model basis. Each object in the list is a that associates a (or model group) with a to try if a ContentPolicyViolationError occurs for the source model.
                • content_policy_fallbacks: [{"gpt-3.5-turbo-small": ["claude-opus"]}] means: "If model returns a ContentPolicyViolationError, retry the request with ."
                • For each source model, you can specify a list of fallback models to try in order. The proxy will attempt the fallbacks sequentially until one succeeds or the list is exhausted.
                • :** The model names used as source models and fallback models must be valid aliases defined in your section.

                In this example, if a request to results in a ContentPolicyViolationError, the proxy will first retry the request with . If that also fails (or also results in a content policy error), it will then try . Separate fallback mappings are defined for to use as a content policy fallback.

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only parameter)

                is specifically for handling ContentPolicyViolationError errors. For other types of errors, use the general or router_settings.fallbacks mechanisms.

                Array of Objects (List of mappings)

                (Empty list, no context window fallbacks configured).

                The parameter, within , is an used to specify fallback models specifically for ContextWindowExceededError errors. This error occurs when the input prompt (or combined prompt and response length) is too long and exceeds the maximum context window of the model.

                • provides a mechanism to automatically handle requests that are too long for a given model's context window. Instead of failing, the proxy can intelligently retry the request with a model that has a larger context window.
                • Model-Specific Fallbacks for Context Window Errors: Similar to , you define fallbacks on a per-model basis. Each object in the list is a that associates a (or model group) with a with larger context windows.
                • context_window_fallbacks: [{"gpt-3.5-turbo-small": ["gpt-3.5-turbo-large", "claude-opus"]}] means: "If model returns a ContextWindowExceededError, first retry with , and if that also fails (or also gets a context window error), then try ."
                • Ordered Fallback Attempts: The proxy will try the fallback models in the list sequentially until a model with a sufficiently large context window is found or the list is exhausted.
                • :** The model names used as source models and fallback models must be valid aliases defined in your .

                In this example, if (perhaps a model with a 4K context window) gets a request that's too long, the proxy will first try (e.g., a 16K context model). If that still fails due to context length, it will then attempt to use (potentially a model with a very large context). A separate fallback is defined for (assuming it has a smaller context than ).

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only parameter)

                is specifically for ContextWindowExceededError errors. For other error types, use the general or router_settings.fallbacks mechanisms.

                disable_end_user_cost_tracking

                disable_end_user_cost_tracking

                (End-user cost tracking is enabled by default).

                The disable_end_user_cost_tracking parameter, within , is a that controls whether the proxy should track costs at the level. By default, LiteLLM Proxy tracks and records the cost of each request, attributing it to the specific end-user (if a identifier is provided in the request or associated with the API key). This per-user cost tracking is used for:

                • Generating dashboards that show LLM spend broken down by user, team, or project.
                • Cost Control and Budgeting: Enforcing budgets and quotas at the user or team level.
                • Providing granular usage data for cost optimization and resource allocation.

                Setting disable_end_user_cost_tracking: true will turn off this per-user cost tracking. The proxy will still track usage and costs, but it will not record or report costs at the individual end-user level.

                • You might disable per-user cost tracking for privacy reasons, if you do not want to associate costs with specific users.
                • Simplified Cost Reporting: If you only need aggregate cost metrics and do not require per-user spend breakdowns, disabling this feature can simplify your data and reduce the amount of data stored in the proxy's database.

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only parameter)

                When end-user cost tracking is disabled, the proxy will still track and report costs (total spend across all requests), but the per-user breakdown will not be available.

                disable_end_user_cost_tracking_prometheus_only

                disable_end_user_cost_tracking_prometheus_only

                (End-user cost tracking is enabled in Prometheus metrics by default).

                The disable_end_user_cost_tracking_prometheus_only parameter, within , is a that provides more granular control over end-user cost tracking. When set to , it disables per-user cost tracking *specifically in Prometheus metrics, but **continues to record per-user cost data in the proxy's database spend logs*.

                • Selective Metric Exposure: This setting allows you to control per-user cost information is exposed. You might use this if:
                  • You want to retain detailed per-user spend logs in your internal database for audit or detailed analysis.
                  • But you do want to expose per-user cost metrics via Prometheus, perhaps for privacy or security reasons when Prometheus metrics are exposed externally or to a wider audience.
                • Database Logs Still Retain Detail: Even when Prometheus tracking is disabled via this flag, the proxy will still write per-user spend information to the database table, ensuring that detailed spend data is still captured internally.

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only parameter)

                disable_end_user_cost_tracking turns off per-user cost tracking (database and Prometheus). disable_end_user_cost_tracking_prometheus_only turns it off only in Prometheus metrics, while keeping detailed per-user logs in the database.

                If not provided, default key generation settings apply (no special restrictions beyond normal authentication).

                The parameter, within , is an (object) that controls who is allowed to generate API keys (virtual keys) via the proxy's admin endpoints (e.g., the API or the Admin UI). This is primarily an enterprise-level configuration, relevant when you expose a UI or API for users or teams to create their own API keys for accessing the proxy.

                • Granular Key Generation Control: allows you to define fine-grained rules and restrictions on API key generation, enabling you to implement policies around who can create keys and what requirements must be met during key creation.
                • The object can contain two sub-keys:
                  • : Settings specifically for generating (keys associated with a team or organization).
                  • : Settings for generating (keys not tied to a team).
                • This is primarily an used in multi-tenant scenarios or when you need to enforce strict governance over API key issuance.

                Configuration Options within Sub-Keys:

                For both and , you can define the following restrictions:

                • allowed_team_member_roles / : (Array of Strings): Lists the user roles that are permitted to generate keys of this type. Roles are typically defined within your user authentication system (e.g., "admin", "proxy_admin", "developer", etc.). Only users with roles listed here will be authorized to create keys. If not specified, there's no role-based restriction beyond general authentication.
                • (Array of Strings): Lists parameters that are to be provided when generating a key of this type. For example, you might require that team admins always provide when creating team API keys for cost tracking purposes. If a required parameter is missing during key generation, the request will be rejected.
                • Only team members with the role can create team API keys, and they provide a parameter when creating a team key.
                • Only users with the or roles can create personal API keys. There are no additional parameter requirements for personal keys.

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only parameter)

                is an primarily relevant for multi-tenant deployments or organizations with strict API key governance policies. If unspecified, there are no special restrictions on key generation beyond the normal authentication requirements of the proxy.

                disable_add_transform_inline_image_block

                The disable_add_transform_inline_image_block parameter, within , is a specifically related to integration with . When set to , it disables the automatic addition of to the parameter for non-vision Fireworks AI models.

                • Fireworks AI Specific Behavior: Fireworks AI's API might require image URLs to include as a query parameter for certain models, especially for image generation calls when the model itself is a vision model.
                • Automatic Parameter Appending (Default): By default (disable_add_transform_inline_image_block: false), LiteLLM Proxy will automatically append to the when making image generation requests to Fireworks AI models that are vision models. This is to ensure compatibility with Fireworks AI's API requirements.
                • Disabling Auto-Addition (Optional): Setting disable_add_transform_inline_image_block: true will turn off this automatic parameter appending. You might want to disable it if:
                  • You are using a Fireworks AI model where this parameter is not needed or causes issues.

                disable_hf_tokenizer_download

                disable_hf_tokenizer_download

                (Hugging Face tokenizer download is enabled by default).

                The disable_hf_tokenizer_download parameter, within , is a that controls whether LiteLLM should attempt to download Hugging Face tokenizer files.

                • Hugging Face Tokenizer Usage (Default): By default (disable_hf_tokenizer_download: false), LiteLLM will try to use the for Hugging Face models. This often involves downloading tokenizer files from the Hugging Face Hub when a Hugging Face model is first used via the proxy. Using the correct tokenizer is essential for accurate token counting and optimal performance with Hugging Face models.
                • Forcing OpenAI Tokenizer (Alternative): Setting disable_hf_tokenizer_download: true instructs LiteLLM to avoid downloading Hugging Face tokenizers. Instead, it will default to using the OpenAI tokenizer for models, including Hugging Face models.
                • Use Cases for Disabling HF Tokenizer Download: You might want to disable Hugging Face tokenizer download in scenarios such as:
                  • In environments with limited or no internet access, preventing tokenizer downloads can be necessary.
                  • In highly secure or restricted environments where downloading external files is prohibited or undesirable.
                  • Startup Speed Optimization: Downloading tokenizer files can sometimes add to the startup time of the proxy, especially on slower networks. Disabling downloads can speed up proxy startup, at the potential cost of less accurate tokenization for Hugging Face models.
                  • If you specifically want to ensure that the OpenAI tokenizer is used for all models, even Hugging Face models, for consistency or compatibility reasons.

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only parameter)

                Important Considerations:

                • Using the OpenAI tokenizer for Hugging Face models may lead to less accurate token counting and potentially suboptimal performance for some models, as Hugging Face models are often trained and optimized with their specific tokenizers.
                • Forcing the OpenAI tokenizer might lead to and unexpected behavior with certain Hugging Face models that heavily rely on their specific tokenization schemes.
                • Default Behavior Recommendation: Unless you have a specific reason to disable Hugging Face tokenizer downloads (like offline environments or forced OpenAI tokenizer usage), it's generally recommended to leave disable_hf_tokenizer_download: false (the default) to ensure proper tokenizer usage for Hugging Face models.

                The parameter, within , is the for enabling or disabling caching of LLM responses within the LiteLLM Proxy Server.

                • Setting turns on caching for the proxy. This means that, by default, the proxy will attempt to cache eligible requests and retrieve cached responses whenever possible, using the cache backend and settings configured in .

                qdrant_quantization_config ( -> Qdrant)

                qdrant_quantization_config

                No quantization configured by default (vectors are stored as full-precision floats).

                The qdrant_quantization_config parameter, within when or enabling semantic cache, allows you to specify a for vector embeddings stored in Qdrant. Quantization is a technique to reduce the memory footprint and potentially improve search speed of vector embeddings in a vector database like Qdrant.

                • Vector Quantization Options: Currently, the documentation mentions support for quantization. Other quantization methods might be supported or added in future versions of LiteLLM.
                  • : Binary quantization reduces the size of vectors by representing them using binary codes (0s and 1s) instead of full-precision floating-point numbers. This can significantly reduce storage space and improve search performance, especially for very large datasets.
                • Quantization typically involves a trade-off between storage/performance gains and potential loss of precision in the vector embeddings. Binary quantization is a more aggressive form of quantization and may result in some loss of accuracy in semantic similarity search compared to using full-precision vectors.
                • qdrant_quantization_config is . If you omit this parameter, vectors will be stored in Qdrant using their original full-precision floating-point representation (no quantization). Quantization is typically used for optimizing storage and performance when dealing with very large caches or when memory/performance is a critical concern.

                Example YAML (Qdrant qdrant_quantization_config):

                In this example, qdrant_quantization_config: "binary" enables binary quantization for vectors stored in the Qdrant cache.

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only parameter)

                Consider using quantization (e.g., ) if you are planning to build a very large semantic cache with Qdrant, where storage space and search performance are critical. If accuracy is paramount and you have sufficient resources, you can omit qdrant_quantization_config to use full-precision vectors. Test and evaluate the impact of quantization on your specific use case to determine the optimal setting.

                ( -> Qdrant)

                N/A (Required when using Qdrant semantic cache)

                The parameter, within when or enabling semantic cache, is a that defines the cosine similarity threshold for determining a cache hit in Qdrant semantic caching.

                • Semantic Similarity Matching: Semantic caching, unlike exact-match caching, retrieves cached responses based on the of the incoming prompt to prompts that are already in the cache. Cosine similarity is a common metric used to measure semantic similarity between vector embeddings.
                • sets the minimum cosine similarity score required for a cached response to be considered a match for an incoming request. The cosine similarity score ranges from 0 to 1, where:
                  • Perfect cosine similarity (vectors are identical in direction).
                  • No cosine similarity (vectors are orthogonal).
                  • Indicate higher semantic similarity.
                  • Indicate lower semantic similarity.
                • Tuning for Cache Hit Rate vs. Relevance: The value directly impacts the and the relevance of cached responses:
                  • Higher threshold (closer to 1.0): Makes semantic matching . Only prompts that are semantically similar to cached prompts will result in a cache hit. This leads to a but potentially of cached responses (as they are very close in meaning to the current request).
                  • Lower threshold (closer to 0.0): Makes semantic matching . Prompts that are semantically similar can trigger a cache hit. This results in a but potentially of cached responses (as they might be less semantically aligned with the current request).
                • Experimentation and Tuning: The optimal value depends on your specific application and use case. You will likely need to this value to find a balance between cache hit rate and the desired level of semantic relevance for cached responses. A typical starting point might be around or .

                Example YAML (Qdrant ):

                In this example, similarity_threshold: 0.8 means that a cached response will only be considered a match if the cosine similarity between the incoming prompt's embedding and a cached prompt's embedding is 0.8 or higher.

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only parameter)

                Start with a around and then adjust it based on your testing and evaluation of cache hit rates and the semantic relevance of retrieved cached responses for your application. Lower the threshold to increase cache hits (potentially at the cost of relevance), and raise it to decrease cache hits but increase relevance.

                The parameter, within , is an that allows you to specify which types of API calls should be cached by the LiteLLM Proxy. This provides granular control over what gets cached and what does not.

                • By default, LiteLLM Proxy is capable of caching several types of API calls:
                  • : Asynchronous chat completion calls (corresponding to endpoint).
                  • : Asynchronous text completion calls ( endpoint).
                  • : Asynchronous embedding calls ( endpoint).
                  • : Asynchronous audio transcription calls ( endpoint).
                • Using , you can customize of these call types are actually cached. For example:
                  • supported_call_types: ["acompletion", "aembedding"]: Only cache chat completions and embeddings, but not text completions or transcriptions.
                  • supported_call_types: ["acompletion"]: Only cache chat completions, and disable caching for all other call types.
                  • : Disable caching for call types, even if is set. This is effectively a way to disable caching selectively without turning off the master switch.
                • Performance Optimization: Use to optimize caching for your specific application's needs. You might choose to cache only the most frequently used or expensive call types, while excluding less critical or less cacheable calls.

                In this example, only asynchronous chat completion () and embedding () calls will be cached. Text completions () and transcriptions () will not be cached, even though caching is generally enabled ().

To enable caching for a request, the client application must explicitly request caching, typically by including a parameter in the API call (e.g., in the request headers or body, depending on the client library).

If no default is documented, it's likely that cache entries might persist indefinitely unless explicitly evicted or the cache backend has its own eviction policies. It's highly recommended to explicitly set a value.

                The parameter, within , sets the time-to-live (TTL) in seconds for cache entries. TTL determines how long a cached response remains valid and will be served from the cache before it is considered .

                • is a fundamental parameter for cache management. It defines the maximum age of a cached response. After the specified TTL duration has elapsed since the response was cached, the cached entry is considered or expired.
                • When a cached response is expired due to TTL, the proxy will for subsequent requests, even if the request matches the cache key. Instead, the proxy will forward the request to the backend LLM provider, get a fresh response, and potentially cache the new response (replacing the expired entry).
                • Balancing Freshness and Performance: The value represents a trade-off between:
                  • Cache Hit Rate and Performance: Longer TTL values (e.g., hours or days) increase the likelihood of cache hits and improve performance, as cached responses remain valid for longer. However, they might serve stale or outdated information if the underlying LLM or data changes frequently.
                  • Data Freshness and Accuracy: Shorter TTL values (e.g., minutes or seconds) ensure that responses are more up-to-date and accurate, reflecting recent changes in the LLM or data, but they reduce the cache hit rate and may increase API costs and latency.
                • The optimal value depends heavily on your application's requirements for data freshness, the frequency of changes in the underlying LLM or data, and your performance/cost optimization goals. For relatively static data or tasks where slightly stale data is acceptable, you can use longer TTL values. For applications requiring highly dynamic or real-time data, use shorter TTL values or disable caching altogether.

                Example YAML (Cache TTL of 10 minutes):

                In this example, cache entries will expire and be considered invalid after 600 seconds (10 minutes) from when they were initially cached.

                Example Environment Variable: N/A (YAML-only parameter)

                Always explicitly set a value that is appropriate for your application's needs. If data freshness is critical, use shorter TTLs. If performance and cost reduction are prioritized and some staleness is acceptable, use longer TTLs. Monitor your cache hit rates and application behavior to fine-tune the value for optimal performance and data accuracy. If no caching is desired for certain data, disable caching for those call types using or disable caching altogether with .

                (Message logging in OpenTelemetry is enabled by default).

                The callback_settings.otel.message_logging parameter, within the top-level section and specifically under the key, is a that controls whether the OpenTelemetry () callback should log message content and responses. This setting provides fine-grained control over what data is included in OpenTelemetry traces specifically for the OTEL callback.

                • OTEL-Specific Message Logging Control: This parameter applies only to the OpenTelemetry logging integration. It does not affect other logging callbacks or the general setting.
                • Message Content in Traces (Default): By default ( or if this parameter is omitted), the OpenTelemetry callback will log the full content of prompts (messages) and responses within the OpenTelemetry traces. This provides detailed context within traces, which is helpful for debugging and understanding the flow of conversations or requests.
                • Disabling Message Content in OTEL Traces: Setting will instruct the OTEL callback not to log message content or responses in the traces it generates. Only high-level metadata (like request parameters, timestamps, etc.) will be included in the OTEL spans.
                • Privacy or Trace Volume Reduction: You might disable message logging in OTEL traces for:
                  • If you are concerned about sensitive data appearing in your OpenTelemetry traces, especially if traces are exported to external systems or accessed by a wider audience.
                  • Omitting message content can significantly reduce the size and volume of OpenTelemetry traces, which can be beneficial for performance in high-throughput systems or when dealing with large trace datasets.

                In this example, OpenTelemetry tracing is enabled for all requests (via ), but the setting under ensures that the actual text content of prompts and responses will be included in the generated OTEL traces. Only metadata will be traced.

In this case, IP-based access control is effectively disabled, and the proxy will accept requests from any source IP address (subject to other authentication mechanisms).
